title: Dog Bleeding Gums After Rodenticide Exposure: Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Bleeding gums after rodenticide exposure is a life-threatening emergency for dogs. Learn why immediate veterinary care is critical for survival.
content_html:
Understanding Rodenticide Exposure and Bleeding Gums

Rodenticide poisoning occurs when a dog ingests bait designed to kill rodents. Most common baits are anticoagulants, which work by blocking the synthesis of Vitamin K, a critical component for blood clotting. When a dog is exposed, their body loses the ability to stop even minor bleeds. If you observe bleeding gums, it is a clinical sign that the toxin has already depleted your dog's clotting factors, leading to spontaneous hemorrhaging.

Urgency Level: High Emergency

This is a critical, life-threatening situation. If your dog is showing signs of bleeding after exposure to rodenticide, they require immediate veterinary intervention. The urgency level is categorized as High because internal bleeding is likely occurring simultaneously in other parts of the body, such as the lungs, chest, or abdomen. Do not wait for the bleeding to stop on its own; without professional treatment, including Vitamin K1 therapy and potentially blood transfusions, this condition is fatal.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear photo of your dog's gums to a veterinary professional can be a vital first step in triage. A photo allows the medical team to assess the severity of the bleeding and the color of the mucous membranes—pale or white gums are a sign of dangerous blood loss or shock. Additionally, if you have the packaging of the rodenticide, a photo of the active ingredients and concentration helps the veterinarian determine the exact treatment protocol and dosage needed to save your pet's life.

faqs.question: How long does it take for symptoms to appear after ingestion?
faqs.answer: Symptoms usually do not appear immediately. It typically takes 3 to 7 days for the dog's stored clotting factors to be depleted before visible bleeding begins.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog Vitamin K supplements from a health store?
faqs.answer: No. Over-the-counter Vitamin K supplements are not the same as the prescription-strength Vitamin K1 (phytonadione) required to treat rodenticide poisoning.

faqs.question: What other symptoms should I watch for?
faqs.answer: In addition to bleeding gums, watch for extreme lethargy, coughing or difficulty breathing, nosebleeds, and dark, tarry, or bloody stools.
